{{wikidata link|Q11418296}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称 = Kanko Shrine | 画像 = [[Image:Kanko-jinja2.jpg|270px|Kanko Shrine]]
Haiden | 所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small), former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{jalink|October 17|Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and a former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{jalink|chinju-sha|Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, with {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{jalink|Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{jalink|Empress Suiko|Q298057}} 2 ({{jalink|594|Q33625}}), {{jalink|Soga no Umako|Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}), on January 8, {{jalink|Kūkai|Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{jalink|Kangiten|Q11545315|Shōten}}, {{jalink|Benzaiten|Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{jalink|Nanboku-chō period|Q264012}}, {{jalink|Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{jalink|Takeyama Castle|Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{jalink|Chōroku|Q1193524}} 4 ({{jalink|1460|Q6528}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{jalink|Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891}} in the {{jalink|Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{jalink|Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341}}' records that in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{jalink|1915|Q2084}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{jalink|kaya grass|Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{jalink|Benzaiten}}, {{jalink|Shitten}}, and {{jalink|Shōten|Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

{{wikidata link|Q11418296}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称 = Kanko Shrine | 画像 = [[Image:Kanko-jinja2.jpg|270px|Kanko Shrine]]
Haiden | 所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small), former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{jalink|October 17|Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and a former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{jalink|chinju-sha|Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, with {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{jalink|Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{jalink|Empress Suiko|Q298057}} 2 ({{jalink|594|Q33625}}), {{jalink|Soga no Umako|Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}), on January 8, {{jalink|Kūkai|Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{jalink|Kangiten|Q11545315|Shōten}}, {{jalink|Benzaiten|Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{jalink|Nanboku-chō period|Q264012}}, {{jalink|Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{jalink|Takeyama Castle|Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{jalink|Chōroku|Q1193524}} 4 ({{jalink|1460|Q6528}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{jalink|Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891}} in the {{jalink|Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{jalink|Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341}}' records that in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{jalink|1915|Q2084}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{jalink|kaya grass|Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{jalink|Benzaiten}}, {{jalink|Shitten}}, and {{jalink|Shōten|Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

{{wikidata link|Q11418296}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称 = Kanko Shrine | 画像 = [[Image:Kanko-jinja2.jpg|270px|Kanko Shrine]]
Haiden | 所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small), former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{jalink|October 17|Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and a former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{jalink|chinju-sha|Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, with {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{jalink|Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{jalink|Empress Suiko|Q298057}} 2 ({{jalink|594|Q33625}}), {{jalink|Soga no Umako|Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}), on January 8, {{jalink|Kūkai|Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{jalink|Kangiten|Q11545315|Shōten}}, {{jalink|Benzaiten|Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{jalink|Nanboku-chō period|Q264012}}, {{jalink|Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{jalink|Takeyama Castle|Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{jalink|Chōroku|Q1193524}} 4 ({{jalink|1460|Q6528}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{jalink|Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891}} in the {{jalink|Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{jalink|Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341}}' records that in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{jalink|1915|Q2084}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{jalink|kaya grass|Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{jalink|Benzaiten}}, {{jalink|Shitten}}, and {{jalink|Shōten|Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

Haiden | 所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small), former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{jalink|October 17|Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and a former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{jalink|chinju-sha|Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, with {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{jalink|Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{jalink|Empress Suiko|Q298057}} 2 ({{jalink|594|Q33625}}), {{jalink|Soga no Umako|Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}), on January 8, {{jalink|Kūkai|Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{jalink|Kangiten|Q11545315|Shōten}}, {{jalink|Benzaiten|Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{jalink|Nanboku-chō period|Q264012}}, {{jalink|Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{jalink|Takeyama Castle|Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{jalink|Chōroku|Q1193524}} 4 ({{jalink|1460|Q6528}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{jalink|Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891}} in the {{jalink|Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{jalink|Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341}}' records that in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. In August {{jalink|1915|Q2084}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{jalink|kaya grass|Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{jalink|Benzaiten}}, {{jalink|Shitten}}, and {{jalink|Shōten|Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

{{wikidata link|Q11418296}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称 = Kanko Shrine | 画像 = [[Image:Kanko-jinja2.jpg|270px|Kanko Shrine]]
Haiden | 所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small), former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{jalink|October 17|Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and a former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{jalink|chinju-sha|Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, with {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{jalink|Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{jalink|Empress Suiko|Q298057}} 2 ({{jalink|594|Q33625}}), {{jalink|Soga no Umako|Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}), on January 8, {{jalink|Kūkai|Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{jalink|Kangiten|Q11545315|Shōten}}, {{jalink|Benzaiten|Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{jalink|Nanboku-chō period|Q264012}}, {{jalink|Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{jalink|Takeyama Castle|Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{jalink|Chōroku|Q1193524}} 4 ({{jalink|1460|Q6528}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{jalink|Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891}} in the {{jalink|Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{jalink|Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341}}' records that in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{jalink|1915|Q2084}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{jalink|kaya grass|Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{jalink|Benzaiten}}, {{jalink|Shitten}}, and {{jalink|Shōten|Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

Haiden | 所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small), former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{jalink|October 17|Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and a former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{jalink|chinju-sha|Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, with {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{jalink|Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{jalink|Empress Suiko|Q298057}} 2 ({{jalink|594|Q33625}}), {{jalink|Soga no Umako|Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}), on January 8, {{jalink|Kūkai|Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{jalink|Kangiten|Q11545315|Shōten}}, {{jalink|Benzaiten|Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{jalink|Nanboku-chō period|Q264012}}, {{jalink|Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{jalink|Takeyama Castle|Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{jalink|Chōroku|Q1193524}} 4 ({{jalink|1460|Q6528}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{jalink|Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891}} in the {{jalink|Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{jalink|Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341}}' records that in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{jalink|1915|Q2084}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{jalink|kaya grass|Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{jalink|Benzaiten}}, {{jalink|Shitten}}, and {{jalink|Shōten|Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]]

Haiden | 所在地 = {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}{{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (small), former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{jalink|October 17|Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{jalink|Tondabayashi|Q490928}}, {{jalink|Osaka Prefecture|Q122723}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} and a former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{jalink|chinju-sha|Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}, with {{jalink|Futodama|Q11059539|Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{jalink|Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{jalink|Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{jalink|Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{jalink|Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{jalink|Takeyama|Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{jalink|Empress Suiko|Q298057}} 2 ({{jalink|594|Q33625}}), {{jalink|Soga no Umako|Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{jalink|Kōnin|Q1373070}} 14 ({{jalink|823|Q29944}}), on January 8, {{jalink|Kūkai|Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{jalink|Kangiten|Q11545315|Shōten}}, {{jalink|Benzaiten|Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{jalink|Gozu Tennō|Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{jalink|Nanboku-chō period|Q264012}}, {{jalink|Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{jalink|Takeyama Castle|Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{jalink|Chōroku|Q1193524}} 4 ({{jalink|1460|Q6528}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{jalink|Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891}} in the {{jalink|Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{jalink|Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341}}' records that in {{jalink|Eishō|Q1193577}} 4 ({{jalink|1507|Q6161}}), with {{jalink|Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{jalink|separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{jalink|Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756}}. In {{jalink|1872|Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{jalink|1909|Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{jalink|1915|Q2084}} ({{jalink|Taishō|Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{jalink|kaya grass|Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{jalink|Benzaiten}}, {{jalink|Shitten}}, and {{jalink|Shōten|Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{jalink|Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

{{wikidata link|Q11418296}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称 = Kanko Shrine | 画像 = [[Image:Kanko-jinja2.jpg|270px|Kanko Shrine]]
Haiden | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Tondabayashi|Q490928|WD=Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{ill|lt=Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756|WD=Q11588756}}, {{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small), former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{ill|lt=Kōnin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} 14 ({{ill|lt=823|Q29944|WD=Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{ill|lt=Tondabayashi|Q490928|WD=Q490928}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and a former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{ill|lt=Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|WD=Q11679279|lt=Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Takeyama|Q11477431|WD=Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{ill|lt=chinju-sha|Q13479654|WD=Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756|WD=Q11588756}}, with {{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} {{ill|lt=separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{ill||lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{ill|lt=Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375|WD=Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{ill|lt=Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|WD=Q11679279|lt=Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{ill|lt=Takeyama|Q11477431|WD=Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057}} 2 ({{ill|lt=594|Q33625|WD=Q33625}}), {{ill|lt=Soga no Umako|Q1191233|WD=Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{ill|lt=Kōnin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} 14 ({{ill|lt=823|Q29944|WD=Q29944}}), on January 8, {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{ill|lt=Kangiten|Q11545315|WD=Q11545315|lt=Shōten}}, {{ill|lt=Benzaiten|Q818468|WD=Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Nanboku-chō period|Q264012|WD=Q264012}}, {{ill|lt=Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234|WD=Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{ill|lt=Takeyama Castle|Q11477432|WD=Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{ill|lt=Chōroku|Q1193524|WD=Q1193524}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1460|Q6528|WD=Q6528}}), with {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676|WD=Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891|WD=Q5680891}} in the {{ill|lt=Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433|WD=Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{ill|lt=Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341|WD=Q10856341}}' records that in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161}}), with {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689|WD=Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{ill|lt=separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{ill|lt=Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756|WD=Q11588756}}. In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{ill|lt=kaya grass|Q11617144|WD=Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{ill||lt=Benzaiten|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Shitten|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|lt=Shōten|Q97379272|WD=Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{ill|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773|WD=Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Haiden | 所在地 = {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}{{ill|lt=Tondabayashi|Q490928|WD=Q490928}}Ryūsen 886 | 位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|27|45|N|135|35|51|E|scale:30000}} | 祭神 = {{ill|lt=Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756|WD=Q11588756}}, {{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} | 社格 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small), former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建 = {{ill|lt=Kōnin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} 14 ({{ill|lt=823|Q29944|WD=Q29944}}) | 本殿 = {{ill|lt=Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781|WD=Q3193781}} | 例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Osaka|label=Kanko Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kanko Shrine''' (こんくじんじゃ; common reading: かんくじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in Ryūsen, {{ill|lt=Tondabayashi|Q490928|WD=Q490928}}, {{ill|lt=Osaka Prefecture|Q122723|WD=Q122723}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} and a former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} under the old shrine-ranking system. It lies north of {{ill|lt=Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|WD=Q11679279|lt=Ryūsen-ji}} on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Takeyama|Q11477431|WD=Q11477431}} (Ryūsenji-yama), and until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} it was Ryūsen-ji's {{ill|lt=chinju-sha|Q13479654|WD=Q13479654}}. === Enshrined deities === The principal deity is {{ill|lt=Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756|WD=Q11588756}}, with {{ill|lt=Futodama|Q11059539|WD=Q11059539|lt=Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto}} jointly enshrined. Until the Edo period, as Ryūsen-ji's guardian shrine, it enshrined {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}}, the local land deity. As seen in 'Kawachi Meisho Zue,' from the Edo period onward Kanko Shrine came to be identified with Ryūsen-ji's Gozu Tennō shrine. During {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} {{ill|lt=separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, it became independent from Ryūsen-ji. In the early-Meiji '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}},' because the popularly stated deity and the title Yasaka Daimyōjin were considered unreliable, and because Komuku-no-agata was identified with this area, the deity was changed (citing '{{ill|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|Q1045362|WD=Q1045362}}') to Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, ancestral deity of the Komuku no Agatanushi. There are other theories on the deity as well. 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū' and 'Kyōdo Chishi' name Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto as principal and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} as associate deity. 'Ōsaka-fushi' says the deity is Ame-no-Koyane-no-Mikoto. Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto was the deity of the Engishiki-listed Kankosabi Shrine, merged in {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42) from Kanami, Tondabayashi. The deity of Kankosabi Shrine is also disputed: '{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmyōchō|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}' says Ame-no-Futodama-no-Mikoto, while 'Tondabayashi City History' gives other possibilities including Kamihasabi-no-Mikoto, ancestor deity of Komuku no Agatanushi (Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto), and ancestor of the Sami clan ({{ill|lt=Toyoki-iri-hiko-no-Mikoto|Q11633375|WD=Q11633375}}). === History === According to temple tradition of neighboring {{ill|lt=Ryūsen-ji (Tondabayashi)|Q11679279|WD=Q11679279|lt=Ryūsen-ji}}, an evil dragon once lived in a pond on the slope of {{ill|lt=Takeyama|Q11477431|WD=Q11477431}} (now the Ryūsen-ji garden pond), harming nearby villagers. In {{ill|lt=Empress Suiko|Q298057|WD=Q298057}} 2 ({{ill|lt=594|Q33625|WD=Q33625}}), {{ill|lt=Soga no Umako|Q1191233|WD=Q1191233}} came by imperial order and performed rites, after which the dragon fled to the heavens; Ryūsen-ji was then founded. Later, the dragon's revenge is said to have dried both the temple pond and foothill water veins. In {{ill|lt=Kōnin|Q1373070|WD=Q1373070}} 14 ({{ill|lt=823|Q29944|WD=Q29944}}), on January 8, {{ill|lt=Kūkai|Q222227|WD=Q222227}} (Kōbō Daishi) visited, prayed for rain, restored water to the pond, and three small islands appeared. He built small shrines on the islands enshrining {{ill|lt=Kangiten|Q11545315|WD=Q11545315|lt=Shōten}}, {{ill|lt=Benzaiten|Q818468|WD=Q818468}}, and Shitten, and enshrined {{ill|lt=Gozu Tennō|Q11570247|WD=Q11570247}} as guardian deity. This is said to be the shrine's beginning. Another account says that the Komuku no Agatanushi, who ruled the area identified as Komuku-no-agata, had enshrined their ancestor Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto, and Ryūsen-ji later incorporated that shrine as its guardian shrine. In the {{ill|lt=Nanboku-chō period|Q264012|WD=Q264012}}, {{ill|lt=Kusunoki Masashige|Q382234|WD=Q382234}} built Ryūsen-ji Castle ({{ill|lt=Takeyama Castle|Q11477432|WD=Q11477432}}) on the mountaintop. During ensuing conflicts, Ryūsen-ji and this shrine were drawn into warfare; except for the Niō gate, halls and treasures were burned. They were rebuilt, but from {{ill|lt=Chōroku|Q1193524|WD=Q1193524}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1460|Q6528|WD=Q6528}}), with {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama Yoshinari|Q11578676|WD=Q11578676}} holding Takeyama Castle and fighting {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama Masanaga|Q5680891|WD=Q5680891}} in the {{ill|lt=Battle of Takeyama Castle|Q11477433|WD=Q11477433}}, damage came again. '{{ill|lt=Tamon-in Nikki|Q10856341|WD=Q10856341}}' records that in {{ill|lt=Eishō|Q1193577|WD=Q1193577}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1507|Q6161|WD=Q6161}}), with {{ill|lt=Hatakeyama Yoshihide|Q11578689|WD=Q11578689}} entrenched there, "the foothills of Takeyama burned daily." In the Edo period, when Ryūsen-ji was restored, this shrine was also rebuilt. With Meiji-era {{ill|lt=separation of kami and buddhas|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, this shrine enshrining Gozu Tennō became independent from Ryūsen-ji. When identified with the Engishiki Kanko Shrine, its deity was changed to {{ill|lt=Kamuyai-mimi-no-Mikoto|Q11588756|WD=Q11588756}}. In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{ill|lt=kaya grass|Q11617144|WD=Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Benzaiten|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shitten|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|lt=Shōten|Q97379272|WD=Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{ill|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773|WD=Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

In {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}} (Meiji 5), it was ranked {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. On December 2, {{ill|lt=1909|Q2057|WD=Q2057}} (Meiji 42), the Engishiki '''Narikosabi Shrine''', formerly tutelary deity of present-day Kanami, Tondabayashi, was merged. In August {{ill|lt=1915|Q2084|WD=Q2084}} ({{ill|lt=Taishō|Q329174|WD=Q329174}} 4), it was designated as a shrine receiving state offerings of food and cloth. === Account in the Ryūsen-ji engi === The origin of the Gozu Tennō shrine is described in "Ryūsen-ji Engi," temple traditionSummary based on 'Kyōdoshi no Kenkyū.'. Kōbō Daishi, passing the foot of Takeyama, asked a village elder for water due to thirst but received only a little. When he asked why, the elder recounted an old legend and said: "Stay here for a while and revive this sacred place; then I too will aid your wish." When Daishi asked who he was, he replied: "I am Gozu Tennō, land deity of this place. I have long awaited your coming," and vanished. When Daishi pushed through the mountain and reached where strange clouds lingered, he found a dried pond in a fairy-like landscape. After ten days of ritual prayer, heavy rain fell at night like a waterfall, and a loud cry of the Dragon King's return was heard three times. At dawn the pond was full and a dragon hole was visible in the water. Three islands had formed. Suddenly a woodsman appeared and offered three bundles of {{ill|lt=kaya grass|Q11617144|WD=Q11617144}}. With that grass, Daishi built shrines on the three islands for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Benzaiten|1=UNKNOWN}}, {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shitten|1=UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|lt=Shōten|Q97379272|WD=Q97379272}}. The woodsman said a small rise behind was an old site of Iō Zensei ({{ill|lt=Yakushi Nyorai|Q854773|WD=Q854773}}). About 30 steps away there were stone walls and a torii. The woodsman said this was the local land deity, then left. Daishi immediately invited that deity and made it the lasting guardian of the area. This is today's Kanko Shrine. === Precinct === * Honden * Heiden * Haiden * Shinmon === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} === External links === {{commons-inline|Category:Kanko-jinja}} {{DEFAULTSORT:かんこしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Kanko Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Supershrine]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kawachi Province]] [[Category:大阪府の神社]] [[Category:鎮守社]] [[Category:祇園信仰]] [[Category:富田林市の建築物]] [[Category:河内国の式内社]] [[Category:大阪府の旧村社]] [[Category:富田林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
